title: Musings on Markets
gist: Damodaran argues that S&P 500 inclusion of SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic would not meaningfully move their stock prices because the inclusion price effect has shrunk to nearly zero over two decades, and he dismantles the active-manager arguments against inclusion (momentum distortion, hidden retail risk) as self-serving given SPIVA data showing active funds underperform their style indices in every category over ten years.

themes.theme: IPO trio and index inclusion debate

insights.claim: SpaceX, Anthropic and OpenAI are three potentially trillion-dollar companies lined up to go public, and a heated debate has emerged over whether these mega-cap, money-losing additions should be included in the S&P 500.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Three trillion-dollar AI/space IPOs are the dominant 2026 market event; index-inclusion mechanics are now a live pricing question for these names.
insights.quote: attention has (rightly) been focused on three potentially trillion dollar companies all lined up to go public
insights.locator: Over the last few weeks, attention has

insights.claim: Opposition to including SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic in the S&P 500 comes from three groups with hidden agendas: bruised active managers, academics claiming to protect retail investors, and politicians attacking billionaires.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Read anti-inclusion commentary as motivated reasoning, not disinterested analysis, when assessing the debate.
insights.quote: I see a combination of hidden agendas and misguided views about investing behind each of the three groups that are most vehemently against inclusions
insights.locator: I am skeptical, since I see

insights.claim: In the week before the SpaceX IPO, S&P announced it would keep its requirement that a company be listed and traded at least a year before becoming eligible for index inclusion, meaning OpenAI and Anthropic must also wait a year after listing.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Inclusion of these names is off the table for at least a year, removing a near-term flow catalyst that some bulls were counting on.
insights.quote: S&P removed some of the suspense in the question of whether the company would be included in the index by announcing that they would stick with their requirement that a company be listed and traded at least a year
insights.locator: In the week prior to the SpaceX IPO

insights.claim: A year after listing, SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic will still be money-losing businesses with business models that are works in progress and corporate governance horror stories.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't expect fundamentals or governance to clean up on an index-eligibility timeline; price will track the AI narrative, not profitability.
insights.quote: the truth is that a year after they list and start trading, these three companies will still be money losing businesses, with business models that are still works in progress and will remain corporate governance horror stories
insights.locator: I am glad that S&P is not changing the rules

insights.claim: S&P needs these three trillion-dollar companies in its index more than they need to be in it, so the companies will not bend to meet costly index requirements and any rule-bending will come from S&P.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Watch for S&P to eventually relax inclusion criteria; the index provider has more to lose from excluding the largest stocks.
insights.quote: S&P needs these companies in its index more than they need to be in the index
insights.locator: The bottom line is that S&P needs

insights.claim: S&P 500 inclusion criteria require a company to be listed at least a year, meet liquidity thresholds via free float, and have positive profits in the four quarters leading into listing.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The positive-profits-and-one-year rules are precisely what disqualify the money-losing AI/space trio in the near term.
insights.quote: caveats on inclusion relating to listing age (listed at least a year), liquidity (measured by looking at shares that are available for investors to trade in the market, i.e., free float) and profitability (positive profits in the four quarters leading into the listing)
insights.locator: Constituents: The first and perhaps most critical

insights.claim: Price weighting, used by older indices like the Dow 30, has no redeeming quality because it measures little of consequence and breaks down right after stock splits.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Treat Dow 30 levels as a weak market proxy; cap-weighted indices like the S&P 500 are more meaningful.
insights.quote: I cannot think of a single redeeming quality to price weighting, since it measures very little of consequence, and suffers from breakdowns, right after stock splits
insights.locator: Weights:  You can have two indices

insights.claim: Because the S&P 500 is float-adjusted, Meta is weighted below its true size since most of Zuckerberg's voting class B shares are excluded, and Walmart is similarly underweighted due to family-controlled non-traded holdings.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Founder/family-controlled mega-caps carry less index weight than their full market cap implies, a subtlety for passive exposure analysis.
insights.quote: Meta punches in below its true weight, since the bulk of class B shares (which are voting shares held by Zuckerberg) are not counted, as does Walmart
insights.locator: The S&P 500, as I noted earlier, uses market capitalization

insights.claim: The S&P 500 closed at 7554.29 on June 15, 2026, against a float-adjusted market capitalization of $63,498.44 billion, yielding index units of 0.1190.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Concrete index level and aggregate cap anchor for the mid-2026 US equity market.
insights.quote: the cumulative float-adjusted market capitalization of the 500 companies in the index was $63,498.44 billion
insights.locator: Consider, for instance, the S&P 500 which ended trading on June 15, 2026

insights.claim: When a trillion-dollar company replaces a one-billion company in the S&P 500, the index level stays unchanged at the moment of replacement because the index units are adjusted, but the index's forward fundamentals shift toward more risk, a near-term earnings hit, and possibly higher long-term growth.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Adding the loss-making AI trio would not jolt the index level but would dilute index earnings and raise index risk going forward.
insights.quote: while the index level will be unchanged by the addition of the trillion dollar company, the other components that it brings with it, including higher growth and perhaps negative earnings, will alter the fundamentals of the index going forward
insights.locator: This will then percolate through into the index earnings

insights.claim: In 2025 aggregated dollar dividends on the S&P 500 were $664.90 billion, which converts to an index dividend of 79.12 using index units of 0.1190.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Hard 2025 dividend figure useful for yield and payout modeling on the index.
insights.quote: the aggregated dollar dividends on the S&P 500 was $664.90 billion, and multiplying that value by the index units (0.1190) yields an index dividend of 79.12
insights.locator: If asked to compute earnings or dividends

themes.theme: Active vs passive and SPIVA evidence

insights.claim: Over the last twenty-five years there have been only three years in which more than 50% of active funds beat the S&P 500, and even those barely, while underperformance in the remaining years was staggering.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The base rate for active large-cap outperformance is dismal; default to low-cost index exposure absent a strong edge.
insights.quote: Over the last twenty five years, there have been three years where more than 50% of active funds have beaten the S&P 500 index, and barely so
insights.locator: Used in that context, one of the most jarring numbers

insights.claim: Under S&P's SPIVA methodology, which compares fund managers to index funds in their own style category, not a single fund group in any style outperformed its respective index past ten years.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Style-adjusted data removes the active manager's main excuse; underperformance is universal across categories over a decade.
insights.quote: there is not a single fund group in any style that outperforms its respective index past ten years
insights.locator: There is very little hope in these numbers

insights.claim: SPIVA results outside the United States show similar active-manager underperformance, undercutting the claim that local market inefficiencies or insider access let foreign managers beat their indices.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Non-US active mandates are not a refuge; passive underperformance findings travel across geographies.
insights.quote: SPIVA also tracks fund manager performance outside the United States, and reports similar results
insights.locator: For those of you who are reading this other geographies

insights.claim: As private equity and hedge funds have grown larger over two decades, their performance has come to resemble that of mutual funds, eroding the thesis that they are smarter than the market.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Scale kills alpha; large alternative allocations from endowments and pensions may not earn their fees.
insights.quote: as they have become larger, they have come to resemble mutual funds, in terms of performance
insights.locator: Thus, two decades ago, the notion that private equity

insights.claim: The S&P 500 includes only 500 of roughly 6000 publicly traded US companies yet represents more than 80% of total US equity market capitalization, making it the single best proxy for aggregate equity performance.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: For aggregate-market exposure the S&P 500 is sufficient; an equal-weighted index better captures the average stock.
insights.quote: the index represents more than 80% of the market capitalization of all US equities
insights.locator: The reason that the S&P 500 has acquired primacy

insights.claim: Vanguard, which listed the S&P 500 index fund in 1976, now offers more than a hundred ETFs and more than two hundred index funds.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Passive product proliferation gives investors granular low-cost access across geographies and factors, deepening the active drain.
insights.quote: Vanguard, a pioneer when it listed the S&P 500 index fund in 1976, now lists more than a hundred ETFs and more than two hundred index funds
insights.locator: Today, you can not only invest in index funds

insights.claim: Facing a choice between a poorly constructed index that index-fund investors would love to trade and a better one they find less attractive, index providers will likely pick the former because of the fees the fund business generates.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Index quality is compromised by fee incentives; scrutinize the construction of trendy indices before allocating.
insights.quote: faced with a choice between a poorly constructed index that index fund investors would love to trade on and a better constructed one that investors find less attractive, it is possible and perhaps even likely that the fund creators will pick the former
insights.locator: The size of the index fund business

insights.claim: The stock-price bump from being added to the S&P 500 has become smaller and more transitory over time and has largely disappeared over the last decade or two, even as funds indexed to the S&P 500 have surged.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't pay up for an inclusion catalyst; the flow-driven pop is essentially gone despite growth in passive assets.
insights.quote: as the value of funds indexed to the S&P 500 has surged over the last two decades, the effect of being added to or taken out of the index has become smaller, not larger
insights.locator: Looking across these studies, there seems to be a consensus

insights.claim: An S&P study of 715 additions and 711 deletions to the S&P 500 between January 1995 and June 2021 found the positive price effects of inclusion and negative effects of removal have depleted over time.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Large-sample evidence backs the disappearing inclusion premium; trade structures betting on index-add pops are obsolete.
insights.quote: S&P took a look at the 715 companies added and 711 company deletions made to the S&P 500 between January 1995 and June 2021
insights.locator: To back this up, I look at one of the most complete studies

insights.claim: There is accumulating evidence that companies added to the S&P 500 are more likely to underperform than outperform in the twelve months after addition.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Inclusion may be a mild contrarian sell signal rather than a buy; fading post-add strength could be a trade.
insights.quote: there is evidence accumulating that companies that get added to the S&P 500 are more likely to underperform than outperform in the twelve months after the addition
insights.locator: Instead, as the value of funds indexed

insights.claim: After Tesla replaced Apartment Investment and Management (AIV) in the S&P 500 on December 18, 2020, Tesla not only underperformed the index in the following months but massively underperformed AIV, the company it replaced.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The marquee Tesla inclusion is a cautionary case study that index-add hype does not translate into outperformance.
insights.quote: Tesla not only under performed the S&P 500 in the months after its inclusion in the index, but massively underperformed the company (AIV) that it replaced in the index
insights.locator: For those who disagree with me

insights.claim: Damodaran predicts the price trajectories of SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic will not be altered by whether they are included in or excluded from the S&P 500.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Position on these names based on AI fundamentals and narrative, not on index-inclusion speculation.
insights.quote: I don't think the price trajectories of any of these firms will be altered by whether they are included in or excluded from the S&P 500
insights.locator: As the debate about whether SpaceX

insights.claim: Inclusion in a widely tracked index like the S&P 500 increases a stock's intraday and short-term volatility but not its price level, because index-fund flows can reverse and momentum cuts both ways.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Passive flows add noise, not durable price support; expect more volatility but no lasting premium from inclusion.
insights.quote: inclusion in a widely-tracked index (like the S&P 500) will increase intraday and short term volatility, but not price levels
insights.locator: Momentum can cut in both directions

insights.claim: Damodaran attributes top-heavy markets and the disappearing small-cap premium not to passive investing but to technology and disruption turning more industries into winner-take-all businesses where a few firms dominate.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Concentration is a fundamentals story, not a flow artifact; the megacap winners earned their dominance via disproportionate earnings.
insights.quote: technology and disruption has changed more industries into winner-take-all businesses, with a few companies dominating these businesses
insights.locator: Winner-take-all economics

insights.claim: Not all large-cap companies have been winners; the winning large caps have delivered a disproportionate share of increased earnings, which argues against the momentum-driven explanation for megacap dominance.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Selectivity within megacaps matters; size alone hasn't won, earnings growth has.
insights.quote: not all large cap companies have been winners, and the winning large cap companies have delivered a disproportionate portion of increased earnings
insights.locator: Looking back at the fading small cap effect

insights.claim: Most active investors and analysts are not seeking out new information or original research but rely on publicly available information and a belief in mean reversion, so only a small subset actually makes prices more informative.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The price-discovery contribution of active management is smaller than claimed; the informative subset will survive the passive shift.
insights.quote: Much of active investing is built around publicly available information and a belief in the power of mean reversion, not original research and seeking information
insights.locator: Active investing and equity research

insights.claim: Doomsday claims that passive investing is making markets less efficient are overwrought, hypocritical and self-serving, since they often come from fund managers who previously dismissed efficient markets as an academic fever dream.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Discount market-efficiency alarmism from active managers as a livelihood-protection argument, not analysis.
insights.quote: they also strike me as both hypocritical and self-serving
insights.locator: Doomsday stories about how passive investing

insights.claim: An endowment or pension manager who steers funds to active managers in the name of preserving market efficiency, while accepting underperformance, would be violating fiduciary responsibility.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The market-efficiency rationale cannot justify fee-laden active mandates for fiduciaries; expect continued passive migration.
insights.quote: any endowment or pension fund manager who uses this argument to steer endowment funds to active money managers would be in violation of his or her fiduciary responsibility
insights.locator: Asking individual investors, retirees and endowment funds

themes.theme: Retail risk, good business vs good investment

insights.claim: An investor in an S&P 500 index fund is far less exposed to underperforming the market than the typical active manager who either over-invests in a name like SpaceX if it falls or avoids it if it rises.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Diversification through an index fund mutes single-name AI/space risk better than concentrated active bets.
insights.quote: an investor in a S&P 500 index fund is far less exposed to underperforming the market than the typical active money manager
insights.locator: Risk and Diversification

insights.claim: The notion that smart money knows whether SpaceX is overvalued and can time investments better is fanciful, because institutional investors are more traders than investors, making them market followers rather than leaders.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't assume institutions have an informational edge on these IPOs; they chase price as much as retail does.
insights.quote: institutional investors are more traders than investors, making them market followers, not leader
insights.locator: Smart and Stupid Money

insights.claim: An investor constrained to only money-making businesses over the last two decades would have underperformed an unconstrained investor even after adjusting for risk, because a money-losing company can be a good investment at the right price and a stable-profit company can be a bad one at the wrong price.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Screening out unprofitable companies is a costly rule; value is about price versus value, not current profitability.
insights.quote: At the right price, a money-losing company can be a good investment and at the wrong price, a company with solid and stable profits can be a bad investment
insights.locator: Good businesses and good investments

insights.claim: More damage has been done to retail and individual investors by those claiming to protect them than by those out to exploit them.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Be wary of paternalistic regulation that restricts retail access to high-volatility names; it can do net harm.
insights.quote: more damage has been done to this group by those claiming try to help and protect them over time than by those who are out to exploit them
insights.locator: I am generally skeptical of attempts to protect

insights.claim: Damodaran says the price paths of SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic will be determined by how the AI story plays out in both substance (growth, unit economics, reinvestment) and perception (hype and momentum).
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Track AI unit economics and reinvestment alongside narrative; both substance and sentiment will drive these stocks.
insights.quote: their price paths will be determined by how the AI story continues to play out in terms of both substance (growth, unit economics, reinvestment) and perception (hype and momentum)
insights.locator: As for the companies (SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic)
